title: The Deal's First Day: US-Iran MOU Takes Effect as Ukraine Strikes Moscow and Hegseth Shakes NATO
subhead: A 14-point memorandum of understanding between the US and Iran entered force on Thursday, starting a 60-day negotiation clock — but Israel's expanded Lebanon occupation, Ukraine's largest drone attack on Moscow, and a Pentagon review of US forces in Europe all underscored how much remains unresolved.

sections.heading: US-Iran Deal Takes Effect, but the Hard Part Starts Now
sections.body:
A 14-point memorandum of understanding signed by President Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian at the Palace of Versailles on Wednesday entered into force on Thursday, triggering a 60-day negotiation window on core issues including Iran's nuclear program, ballistic missiles, and the sequencing of sanctions relief. Vice President JD Vance, positioning himself as the deal's public face, told reporters at the White House that Iran's frozen assets — which he valued at 'north of $200 billion' — would only be released as Iran meets its obligations.

The deal's gaps are already visible. According to NPR, which published the full text of the MOU, the document states only that Iran's stockpile of near-weapons-grade uranium must be 'adequately addressed' — without specifying how. IAEA inspectors have not independently verified the condition or location of Iran's enriched uranium since US-Israeli bombing campaigns last year. Trump himself appeared to downplay the uranium question at the G7, referring to it as 'nuclear dust' and suggesting its practical value may be limited. Critics, including Republican Senators Bill Cassidy, Thom Tillis, and Ted Cruz, have questioned whether the framework imposes fewer restrictions than the 2015 Obama-era JCPOA, which capped uranium at 300 kg of low-enriched material and mandated IAEA snap inspections. The new MOU does not clearly restore those terms.

The deal also includes a reported $300 billion reconstruction plan for Iran and lifts US sanctions — including those tied to UN Security Council resolutions — in exchange for Hormuz reopening. Vance said Iran selling 'a few million dollars worth of oil' would not transform its economy, which he described as in 'freefall' with 'sky high inflation.' He added that sanctions could be reimposed if Iran fails to comply.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first formal agreement to end the US-Iran war that began on February 28 — but it is a memorandum of understanding, not a treaty, and the hardest negotiations on nuclear limits, missile restrictions, and verification have not yet begun. The 60-day clock creates pressure on all sides, and the deal's vagueness on uranium is a significant gap.

sections.heading: Hormuz Reopening Sends Oil Prices to Three-Month Low
sections.body:
Brent crude fell more than $1 per barrel on Thursday, extending a decline that has pushed prices roughly 5% lower over two consecutive sessions to a three-month low. The catalyst: the US-Iran MOU includes an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-third of globally traded oil passes. Reuters reports that Middle Eastern crude prices are expected to fall further if the Strait formally reopens on Friday, releasing millions of barrels of oil stranded in the Gulf for 111 days.

Shipping giants caution that the Strait will not immediately return to normal. Insurance rates will reflect whether navigation is deemed safe, and the physical logistics of clearing a backlog of tankers after months of closure will take time. IEA chief Fatih Birol welcomed the deal but called for Hormuz to reopen 'without conditions.' US gas prices fell below $4 per gallon for the first time since March, according to AAA data cited by WESH.

For Gulf states that rushed to plan alternative pipeline corridors to Red Sea ports during the closure, the deal creates a strategic dilemma: abandon costly long-term infrastructure projects, or continue building redundancy against future disruptions.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz reopening is the most immediate market impact of the deal. Oil prices are the most visible barometer, but the real test is whether commercial shipping insurers and operators deem the Strait safe enough to transit — and whether Iran will charge tolls, as its chief negotiator indicated during the G7.

sections.heading: Israel Draws Expanded Lebanon Zone, Challenging the Iran Pact
sections.body:
Israel published a map on Thursday showing an expanded military control zone in southern Lebanon, directly challenging the terms of the US-Iran pact that calls for Lebanon's sovereignty to be respected. According to Reuters, Israel said it would not rule out carrying out attacks beyond the newly demarcated zone. The move came hours after the MOU took effect, signaling that Israel intends to define its own security perimeter regardless of the deal's terms.

Vice President Vance addressed the Israeli criticism directly, calling it a 'weird panic' and a 'freakout' in a New York Times interview. He said that Trump is 'the only head of state in the entire world who is sympathetic to the nation of Israel at this moment,' adding that anyone in Israel who thinks their biggest problem is the US president 'needs to wake up and smell the reality.' Axios reported that Prime Minister Netanyahu was 'fuming' over the deal.

Israel has seized roughly 386 square miles of territory from neighboring countries — Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza — since October 2023, according to an Associated Press analysis. The expanded Lebanon zone is the latest extension of that pattern, and it directly complicates the US-Iran deal's provisions for Lebanese sovereignty.
sections.whyItMatters: Israel's territorial moves in Lebanon are the most visible challenge to the Iran deal's regional framework. If Israel continues to expand its occupation zone, Hezbollah has a pretext to resume operations, which could pull the US back into the conflict and collapse the MOU.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits Moscow With War's Largest Drone Attack
sections.body:
Ukraine launched one of the largest drone attacks of the war against Moscow on Thursday, striking the Moscow Oil Refinery for the second time in a week and disrupting commercial flights at four Moscow-area airports. The refinery, which accounts for over a third of the capital region's fuel supply, caught fire with thick black smoke visible across the city, according to social media footage confirmed by Russian officials.

Zelenskyy framed the strike as retaliation for a Russian attack that damaged a historic monastery in Kyiv this week. 'If Ukraine burns, your Moscow will burn,' he said in a voice message to reporters. Russian authorities said their air defenses shot down 555 Ukrainian drones overnight, with nearly 200 intercepted near Moscow — roughly double the number of drones Russia launched at Ukraine in the same period, according to the Ukrainian air force.

The attack came hours after Zelenskyy held a coordination call with Trump and Macron at the G7. He said Ukraine had won key pledges of further support from world leaders. The G7 leaders' statement bolstered Kyiv's leverage in potential peace talks with Moscow, Reuters reported. Meanwhile, the IEA said Ukrainian attacks pushed Russian crude production down approximately 5% year-on-year in May to 8.7 million barrels per day — about 10% below target — and Russia has been forced to relax fuel quality standards to stave off domestic shortages, according to Kommersant.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is demonstrating that it can reach deep into Russia's capital and its energy infrastructure at scale. Combined with G7 diplomatic backing and measurable damage to Russian oil output, this shifts the calculus for any future negotiations — Kyiv is showing it will not accept terms from a position of weakness.

sections.heading: Hegseth Announces US Military Review for Europe, Scolds NATO Allies
sections.body:
US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced a six-month Pentagon review of American military forces in Europe at a NATO meeting in Brussels on Thursday, while sharply criticizing allied nations over defense spending and their response to the Iran war. Hegseth called it 'shameful' that European allies refused to give US forces access to bases during the Iran conflict, according to multiple reports.

The review accompanies immediate cuts: the US is reducing deployed fighter jets in Europe from roughly 150 to 100, withdrawing eight aerial refueling tankers, cutting surveillance aircraft from 26 to 15, and reallocating an aircraft carrier, a submarine, and other warships away from European operations. Approximately 5,000 troops will be withdrawn from Germany over the next 6-12 months, and a planned brigade rotation of about 4,000 troops to Poland and Romania has been canceled. Brigade combat team deployments in Europe will decrease from four to three. The stated goal is to revert the US footprint to something close to 2021 levels, before the post-Ukraine invasion buildup.

NATO is now reviewing its own defense strategy in response. The timing — immediately after the G7 summit and the Iran MOU — signals that the Trump administration views the European security architecture as something allies should fund and manage more independently.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant reduction of US military commitment to Europe since the Cold War buildup. The cancellation of the Poland-Romania brigade rotation removes a key deterrent posture against Russia. European nations now face a clear choice: increase defense spending substantially or accept a thinner security umbrella.

sections.heading: G7 Sets Critical Minerals Alliance; China Pushes Back
sections.body:
G7 leaders at the Évian summit agreed to establish a critical minerals cooperation platform and coordinate stockpiling of rare earth elements, with an ambitious target to cap China's share of global rare earth supply at 60% by 2030. China currently accounts for roughly 70% of global rare earth mining and about 90% of processing capacity, according to Newsweek's analysis. The G7 statement cited 'non-market policies and practices' in the critical minerals sector as a threat to member states' economic and national security.

China responded on Thursday. Reuters reported that Beijing defended its export controls on critical minerals and urged G7 nations to respect international trade rules. The response came two days after new Chinese regulations took effect that bring mineral resources further under state control, establishing a more comprehensive reserve system and granting the State Council greater authority over exploration, mining, processing, storage, and trade of designated minerals.

Trump failed to secure European backing for a Pentagon-drafted plan to establish floor prices for critical minerals — a scheme developed with AI assistance that would account for Chinese cost distortions. European governments instead called for studies before committing to such arrangements. The G7 pledged a road map by the end of 2026.

Separately, the Anthropic AI export dispute loomed over the summit. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick issued export controls barring foreign nationals from using Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, citing a reported jailbreak that could extract vulnerability data. Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei, sitting across from Trump at a G7 working lunch, urged leaders to 'resist the temptation to splinter' on AI regulation. Macron warned that if the US can 'turn off the switch' on AI models from one day to the next, it damages trust in the multitrillion-dollar companies leading the field. The Politico report noted the administration's move could be legally challenged, as applying export controls to software access by foreign nationals inside the US is untested legal territory.
sections.whyItMatters: The critical minerals confrontation is a new front in the US-China strategic competition. Beijing's new regulations consolidate state control over the very resources the G7 is trying to diversify away from — and China's 90% processing dominance means diversification will take years. The AI export fight adds a technological dimension: if the US restricts its most advanced AI models from allies, it pushes those allies toward Chinese or European alternatives.

bottomLine: June 18 marked the first full day of the US-Iran MOU, and the limits of the deal were already visible. The 60-day negotiation clock is running, but Israel immediately challenged the pact by expanding its Lebanon occupation zone, Iran's uranium stockpile remains unverified, and Vance's defense of the deal acknowledged that sanctions relief is conditional and reversible. Meanwhile, Ukraine demonstrated that it can strike Moscow at scale, hitting the capital's main oil refinery twice in one week. Hegseth's announcement of a US force review for Europe — with immediate cuts to fighter jets, troops, and naval assets — signals that the Trump administration is reshaping the transatlantic security relationship regardless of what happens with Iran. And at the G7, the critical minerals alliance against China and the Anthropic AI export controls dispute revealed two new axes of strategic competition: resources and technology. The deal stopped the shooting. Everything else is still in play.
